task: I just got a repair estimate with a couple of extra charges on it, and I want to understand what to do before I say yes to anything. What’s the best way to review it and handle it if some of the items don’t match what was discussed?

turns.agent_response:
Best approach: review it line by line before approving anything, and only agree to work that matches the estimate or was clearly authorized.

What to do:
1. Compare the estimate to what was discussed  
   - Mark each item as: agreed, unclear, or not discussed.
   - Check quantities, labor, materials, trip fees, disposal, permits, and “extra” charges.

2. Ask for a breakdown of anything that changed  
   - If an item wasn’t in the original estimate, ask why it’s needed and what it costs.
   - Request written approval for any added work before they do it.

3. Don’t feel rushed  
   - It’s reasonable to say you need time to review the estimate.
   - Don’t approve extra charges just because the work is already underway.

4. Pay only for the agreed work  
   - If some charges don’t match the estimate or approved work, tell them clearly you’ll pay the agreed amount and dispute the rest until it’s clarified.

A simple message you can send:
> I reviewed the estimate and some charges don’t match what we discussed or what was approved. Please send a detailed breakdown of the extra items and any written approval for added work. I’ll review it before approving payment.

If you want, I can help you turn your estimate into a quick “approve / question / reject” list.

user_simulator.text:
Close enough, but I’d want it tighter.

Before I say yes to anything, I’d mark the whole thing three ways:
- agreed to
- not discussed
- flat-out extra

Then I’d ask for it in writing. Not some handyman story over the phone. I want:
- what changed
- why it changed
- the price for each extra item
- whether it was authorized before they did it

If they already did the work and never cleared it with me, that’s on them. I’d still pay the part that matches the estimate, and hold the rest until they explain it. Same way I handled a plumber years ago — if it wasn’t on the estimate, I made them show me why it belonged there.

If you want, I can help you make a short reply that sounds firm without inviting an argument.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right approach.

A tight way to handle it:

- Mark each line item as:
  - agreed to
  - not discussed
  - flat-out extra

- Ask for everything in writing:
  - what changed
  - why it changed
  - the price of each extra item
  - whether you approved it before the work was done

- If they already did the extra work without clearing it with you:
  - pay the part that matches the estimate
  - hold the rest until they explain it

A short reply you can send:

> I reviewed the estimate, and some charges don’t match what we discussed or approved. Please send a written breakdown of the extra items, why they were needed, and whether they were authorized before the work was done. I’ll pay the amount that matches the estimate and review the rest once it’s clarified.

If you want, I can make that even firmer or more casual.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s tight enough.

I’d use it. Maybe even cut the last line if I didn’t feel like being polite. The important part is: get it in writing, separate the agreed part from the extra nonsense, and don’t hand over more than you owe until they explain themselves.

That’s the part people skip, and then they get soaked.
